coredata.dc:title: Nonradiative electron-hole recombination dynamics in edge-selective phosphorus-doped graphene quantum dots for energy conversion applications
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2022 International Solar Energy Society. Published by Elsevier Ltd. All rights reserved.
coredata.prism:publisher: International Solar Energy Society. Published by Elsevier Ltd.
coredata.dc:creator: Cui, Peng
Xue, Yuan
coredata.dc:description: Doping with heteroatoms is an effective way to modify the electronic structures and photophysical properties of graphene quantum dots (GQDs). However, there are few studies on phosphorus-doped GQDs (P-GQDs), especially with respect to the dynamics of electron-hole recombination, which plays a crucial role in the energy conversion performance of P-GQDs. In the current study, we investigate the effect of phosphorus-containing groups (i.e., phosphine oxide, phosphonic acid, phosphate, and phosphinic acid) on the electronic structures, optical properties, and dynamics of nonradiative recombination of P-GQDs with different edge coverages and structural symmetries. Our results show that P-doping disrupts the integrity of the conjugated π-skeleton of carbon system, leading to the localization of electrons in P-GQDs. The phosphine oxide has a stronger effect on the sp2 bonding environment of the carbon skeleton, leading to a greater impact on the geometrical and electronic structures of the P-GQDs than in the other P-functionalized systems. The increase in the passivation density of phosphinic acid leads to localization of electron and hole densities at the periphery of the GQDs. In addition, the PC bond in phosphonic acid dramatically increases the electronic coupling, leading to the fastest non-radiative decay rate. The overall photovoltaic performance of phosphate- and phosphine oxide-functionalized GQDs are better than the other P-functionalized GQDs. Our calculations provide guidance for the rational design of P-doping configurations in P-GQDs to improve their energy conversion efficiency.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Density functional theory
Phosphorous doping
Graphene quantum dot
Energy conversion
Nonradiative recombination dynamics

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Electronic structure and optical properties of P-functionalized GQDs
Energy conversion performance of P-functionalized GQDs

xocs:cp-notices.xocs:cp-notice: © 2022 International Solar Energy Society. Published by Elsevier Ltd. All rights reserved.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a)-(g) Schematic representations of the structures of pristine and P-GQDs.
(a)-(t) Top and side views of P-GQDs with different structural symmetry and edge coverage.
The HOMO-LUMO gap of pristine and functionalized GQDs with various P functional group attached to the edges.

Absorption spectra of P-GQDs with different structural symmetries and edge coverages. The main absorption peaks are indicated by the black arrows, while the low absorption peaks are indicated by the green arrows. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

The calculated non-radiative (
k
nr
(
×
10
9
s
- 1
)) and radiative (
k r
(
×
10
9
s
- 1
)) recombination rates and related parameters for the P-GQDs.
The calculated open circuit voltage (Voc), light harvesting efficiency (LHE) and driving force (ΔGinject).

head.ce:title: Nonradiative electron-hole recombination dynamics in edge-selective phosphorus-doped graphene quantum dots for energy conversion applications

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Nanotechnology Research Laboratory, School of Materials Science and Engineering, Liming Vocational University, Quanzhou, Fujian Province, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Materials Science and Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Nanotechnology Research Laboratory, School of Materials Science and Engineering, Liming Vocational University, Quanzhou, Fujian Province, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Nanotechnology Research Laboratory, No. 1800 Lihu Street, Jiangnan University, Wuxi, Jiangsu Province, PR China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Nanotechnology Research Laboratory, No. 1800 Lihu Street, Jiangnan University, Wuxi, Jiangsu Province, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Doping with heteroatoms is an effective way to modify the electronic structures and photophysical properties of graphene quantum dots (GQDs). However, there are few studies on phosphorus-doped GQDs (P-GQDs), especially with respect to the dynamics of electron-hole recombination, which plays a crucial role in the energy conversion performance of P-GQDs. In the current study, we investigate the effect of phosphorus-containing groups (i.e., phosphine oxide, phosphonic acid, phosphate, and phosphinic acid) on the electronic structures, optical properties, and dynamics of nonradiative recombination of P-GQDs with different edge coverages and structural symmetries. Our results show that P-doping disrupts the integrity of the conjugated π-skeleton of carbon system, leading to the localization of electrons in P-GQDs. The phosphine oxide has a stronger effect on the sp2 bonding environment of the carbon skeleton, leading to a greater impact on the geometrical and electronic structures of the P-GQDs than in the other P-functionalized systems. The increase in the passivation density of phosphinic acid leads to localization of electron and hole densities at the periphery of the GQDs. In addition, the PC bond in phosphonic acid dramatically increases the electronic coupling, leading to the fastest non-radiative decay rate. The overall photovoltaic performance of phosphate- and phosphine oxide-functionalized GQDs are better than the other P-functionalized GQDs. Our calculations provide guidance for the rational design of P-doping configurations in P-GQDs to improve their energy conversion efficiency.

ce:section.ce:para: Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) have attracted much attention due to their immense potential for photodetectors (Konstantatos et al., 2012), bioimaging (Chung et al., 2021), solar conversion (Liu et al., 2020), and photocatalysis (Zeng et al., 2018). They are promising for biomarkers (Hasanzadeh and Shadjou, 2017), solar photovoltaic devices (Teymourinia et al., 2018; Dutta et al., 2012), and photoelectrocatalysis (Zeng et al., 2018; Zhuo et al., 2012), among other applications. Heteroatom doping has been shown to be an effective strategy to significantly improve solar energy conversion efficiency (Liu et al., 2020; Yan et al., 2019; Majumder and Mondal, 2016). For example, ZnO nanorods sensitized by N-doped GQDs (N-GQDs) have shown improved energy conversion efficiency due to the higher photoabsorption in the visible region of N-GQDs (Majumder and Mondal, 2016). Further improvement is achieved by doping GQDs with different elements. For example, the use of S,N-doped GQDs significantly increases the energy conversion efficiency of ZnO nanorods (Majumder et al., 2017).

Electron-hole recombination is the main factor affecting the energy conversion efficiency of GQDs. During photorelaxation, the nonradiative transition decreases the density of charge carriers, which is detrimental to energy conversion. The nonradiative recombination can be tuned by chemical functionalization and heteroatom doping of the GQDs. In the first case, the flexibility of the GQDs is changed by the rigidity of the impurity molecules. Moreover, the electron-phonon coupling is altered due to the electron localization induced by the chemical functionalization. Increasing the stiffness of the GQD structure reduces the overlap of the vibronic wave functions and thus decreases the nonradiative decay, while stronger electron-phonon coupling accelerates the nonradiative decay. For example, Shen et al. reported that the use of polyethylene glycol as a passivator increases the quantum yield (QY) of GQDs to 28 % (Shen et al., 2012). Cui et al. performed density functional theory calculations (DFT) to study the dynamics of nonradiative decay in carboxyl- and carbonyl-functionalized GQDs (Cui and Xue, 2022). They found that the addition of a carboxyl group at the GQD edge leads to a localization of electron density in the carbon skeleton, which increases the electron-phonon coupling of the GQDs and leads to faster nonradiative relaxation.

From a synthetic point of view, doping with heteroatoms is a more accessible way to modify the electronic structures and optical properties of GQDs (Kharangarh et al., 2020). In particular, P doping is energetically more favorable than N doping due to the lower formation energy (i.e., 7.1 eV/atom versus 8.0 eV/atom) (Denis, 2010; Panchakarla et al., 2009). Substitutional doping of impurity atoms into the graphene lattice breaks the integrity of the conjugated π-system and leads to the localization of sp2 electrons in the carbon skeleton. The localization of the electrons alters the electronic structures of the GQDs and modifies the nonradiative decay as well as the QYs. For example, Kharangarh et al. synthesized N-GQDs based on the precursor (NH4)2SO4 and obtained a QY of 49.8 % (Kharangarh et al., 2017). Singh et al. reported that S-doping increased the QYs of GQDs to 53 % (Kharangarh et al., 2018). Noor et al. reported that pyrrolic N facilitated the nonradiative recombination of electrons and holes of N-GQDs (Noor et al., 2018), while Permatasari found the opposite (Permatasari et al., 2016). The contradictory observation is caused by the variation of doping configurations such as elemental content, defect, local bonding environment, and polarizability, which leads to different extent of electron localization in N-GQDs. Therefore, unravel the role of doping configuration in nonradiative decay dynamics of GQDs is critical for improving the photovoltaic performance of GQDs.

Theoretically, the bandgap (0.53 eV) of P-doped graphene is higher than that (0.14 eV) of N-doped graphene at the same doping concentration (Denis, 2010). The larger bandgap effectively reduces the probability of nonradiative electron-hole recombination and increases the short-circuit current density and thus the energy conversion efficiency of P-doped graphene compared to N-doped graphene. As p-type semiconductors, P-doped GQDs (P-GQDs) can form a p-n junction with TiO2 to facilitate charge separation due to the built-in electric field pointing from TiO2 to the P-GQDs (Guo et al., 2020). P-doping configurations play an important role in the physicochemical properties of GQDs, similar to the N-doping configurations in N-GQDs. Li et al. investigated the radical scavenging activity of P-GQDs (Li et al., 2017). They found that the C3PO structure can form PO bonds with ∙OH radicals, which promotes the scavenging efficiency of P-GQDs. Yang et al. investigated the oxygen reduction reaction of P-GQDs and found that the OPC3 structure exhibited higher catalytic activity and stability than other structures (Yang et al., 2017). Furthermore, hen P-GQDs and TiO2 are combined, a p-n junction structure is formed that significantly reduces the non-radiative recombination of charge carriers generated by light, allowing more charge carriers to participate in the photocatalytic reaction (Guo et al., 2020).

X-ray spectroscopic studies (Li et al., 2017; Hasegawa et al., 2015; Xia et al., 2017) have shown that there are four types of P functionalities at the edges of graphene, namely phosphine oxide, phosphinic acid, phosphonate, and phosphonic acid. Therefore, it is very important to understand the specific role of these P-functionalities on the dynamics of nonradiative decay. Experimentally, it is very difficult to study these P functionalities individually because the current synthetic route does not allow rational control of the functionalization of the GQDs. To gain insight into the role of these P functionalities in the physicochemical properties and to interpret the mechanism of energy conversion efficiency of P-GQDs, it is necessary to apply a theoretical approach.

Two important methods for modeling the nonradiative electron-hole recombination dynamics of nanomaterials are ab initio nonadiabatic molecular dynamics (NAMD) (Zheng et al., 2019; Tapavicza et al., 2013) and Fermi's golden rule (Cui and Xue, 2021; Cui and Xue, 2022). The former is based on the solution of the time-dependent Schrödinger equation, while the latter is based on the solution of the time-dependent perturbation theory. For example, Long et al. used the NAMD method to study the charge injection of GQDs into TiO2 (Long et al., 2017). They found that the electron transfer from the lowest unoccupied molecular orbital (LUMO) of GQDs to the conduction band minimum (CBM) of TiO2 occurs in the femtosecond range and is much faster than the reverse electron transfer in the picosecond range. Tong et al. used Fermi's golden rule approach to study the photophysical properties of a series of cyclometallated gold(III)aryl acetylide complexes. They found that the additional triplet minimum of the cyclometallated gold(III)-arylacetylide complex enhances the Franck-Condon overlap and accelerates the nonradiative decay (Tong et al., 2015). Comparing the two approaches, Fermi's golden rule does not rely on the explicit nuclear trajectories that are central to the NAMD approach. The parameters within the rate expression can be easily obtained from density functional theory calculations (DFT), which require only a fraction of the computational effort of NAMD. Therefore, the Fermi's golden rule approach is often used to calculate the nonradiative decay rates of conjugated molecules. However, there are very few reports on the application of this approach to GQDs (Cui and Xue, 2021; Cui and Xue, 2022).

In this study, we investigate the electronic structures and optical properties of GQDs with different P functionalities capped at the edges. The aim of this research is to find out the relationship between the doping configuration and the photophysical properties of P-GQDs, as well as the role of P-functionalities in the non-radiative decay dynamics in GQDs. The effects of edge-selective phosphorus doping on the energy conversion performance of P-GQDs are elucidated by these findings, which are important for photocatalytic and photovoltaic applications.

We constructed the initial structures of P-GQDs by placing a number of P-containing groups at different positions of the GQD edges (Fig. 1 ). Doping with heteroatoms in the basal plane could create defects and change the geometric structures. For example, edge doping has been reported to be energetically more favorable than center doping in S,N-codoped GQDs (Mombrú et al., 2019). In particular, we constructed structures with different C/P ratios, i.e., with different edge coverage of the GQDs. The symmetry of GQDs is altered by the presence of P functional groups at the edges. This motivates us to investigate the variation of the electronic structures of the P-GQDs under different edge coverages and binding sites of the P functional groups. In naming these structures, we use Arabic numerals to represent the edge coverage of P functional groups on the GQD edges. The P functional group is represented by the letter after the numeral. An asymmetric structure is denoted by the prime symbol. In total, five different structures with different binding configurations of the P functional groups were created. GQD-2A and GQD-4A have almost symmetric structures, but GQD-2A′ and GQD-4A′ have asymmetric structures. In addition, two pristine GQDs were generated for comparison, namely
GQD
S
P
and
GQD
L
P
. The first is an undoped GQD with the same size as the P-doped GQDs. The other one is an undoped GQD that is similar to the experimental sample.

By breaking CC double bonds and consequently the integrity of the conjugated π system, doping with heteroatoms affects the local bonding environment of GQDs, leading to localization of electron density at the doping sites. As a result, the electronic structures and optical properties of the functionalized GQDs are affected by the different binding arrangement of the P functional groups at the GQD edges. This also leads to changes in the two lowest optically active states (S3 and S4) and the two dark states (S1 and S2), both of which are doubly degenerate in pristine GQDs (Sheely et al., 2021). The incorporation of P functional groups at the edges of GQDs leads to changes in the frontier molecular orbitals (FMOs) of the GQDs and breaks the degeneracies of these states. The influence of the P functional groups on the FMOs depends on the structural symmetry and edge coverage of the functionalized GQDs.

According to previous studies (Zhang et al., 2012; He et al., 2016), the energy conversion performance of a light sensitizer depends on the open-circuit voltage (Voc), short-circuit current density (Jsc), and fill factor (FF) (Zhang et al., 2012; Zhao et al., 2021), the calculation details of which can be found in the supplementary material, section I. Based on equations (S1)-(S4), the energy conversion efficiency is determined by the Voc, the FF, the light harvesting efficiency (LHE), the driving force (ΔGinject) of electron injection from the excited states of the GQDs to TiO2, and the nonradiative recombination time (trelax) in the GQD. In particular, the fast nonradiative recombination of electrons and holes leads to a larger open-circuit voltage loss and a reduction in the short-circuit current density, and therefore has a detrimental effect on the performance of the photovoltaic device. A larger Voc corresponds to less recombination of charge carriers in the device, improving the energy conversion efficiency. A higher LHE indicates that a high fraction of the solar spectrum can be absorbed, which is beneficial for improving the photon-to-electron conversion efficiency. In addition, a larger ΔGinject of GQD improves the charge injection efficiency from the lowest unoccupied molecular orbital (LUMO) of GQD to the conduction band minimum (CBM) of TiO2.

The nonradiative decay rate is given by the Fermi’s golden rule as:
(1)
k
nr
=
2 π
ℏ
V
if
'
2
F
Δ G , λ
Here,
V
if
'
is the electronic coupling between the initial and final states;
F
Δ G , λ
is the thermally averaged Franck-Condon factor,
Δ G
is the free energy change which is equivalent to the emission energy in nonradiative relaxation (Cui and Xue, 2022); λ is the reorganization energy as explained below.

Considering the high-frequency vibrational modes, the Franck-Condon factor can be written as follows:
(2)
F
Δ G , λ
=
1
4 π λ
k B
T
∑ i
e
-
S
eff
(
S
eff
)
i
i !
× exp
-
Δ G + λ + i ℏ
ω
eff
2
4 λ
k B
T
Here,
S
eff
is the effective Huang-Rhys factor; i represents vibrational energy level;
ω
eff
represents the averaged vibrational frequency over the high-frequency modes (>1000
c m
- 1
); λ is the reorganization energy including contribution from low-frequency intramolecular vibration and solvent polarization; T is the temperature of environment;
k B
is the Boltzmann constant; ℏ is the Planck constant.

Usually Eq. (2) is used in combination with Eq. (1) to explain the experimentally determined nonradiative decay rate of semiconductors. The electronic coupling can be determined approximately. The ΔG can be measured by experimental means. Therefore, the Franck-Condon factor is determined by numerical fitting. However, this approach lacks a detailed description of the influence of the GQD structures, such as edge coverage and structural symmetry, on the nonradiative decay rates. Therefore, in order to determine the exact structure-property relationship, it is necessary to investigate the nonradiative decay dynamics of GQDs using theoretical modelling.

The Huang-Rhys factors of the individual vibrational modes are added to obtain the effective Huang-Rhys factor:
(3)
S
eff
=
∑ i
S i
Here,
S i
is the Huang-Rhys factor of the specific vibrational modes (>1000 cm−1) and is calculated from the equilibrium shift between the optimized geometries for the ground state and the excited state, as described in Ref (Cui and Xue, 2022).

The effective vibrational frequency is given by:
(4)
ω
eff
=
κ
ℏ
S
eff
Here, κ is the effective reorganization energy of high-frequency vibrational modes and can be expressed as:
(5)
κ =
∑ i
S i
ℏ
ω i
Here,
ω i
is the high frequency vibrational frequency and can be obtained by frequency calculations implemented in the Gaussian09 software package (Frisch et al., 2009).

The intramolecular reorganization energy is determined by summation over the low-frequency vibrational modes using Eq. (5). The reorganization energy of the solvent is determined using a state-specific approach as described below.

The electronic polarization of the solvent progresses much faster than the nuclear polarization of the solvent in a nonradiative relaxation in the Franck-Condon region and is therefore determined by the final state of the solute, whereas the nuclear polarization is determined by the initial state of the solute. Under such condition, the solvent is in a non-equilibrium state. On the other hand, if both the electronic and nuclear polarizations are determined by the initial or final state of the solute, the solvent is in equilibrium. Consequently, the emission energy is the energy difference between the excited state calculated in the equilibrium solvent and the ground state calculated in the nonequilibrium solvent (Tong et al., 2015). Similarly, the reorganization energy of the solvent is the polarization energy required for the nucleus of the solvent to adapt to the new electronic potential of the final solution state. Thus, it is the difference in energy between the ground states estimated with nonequilibrium and equilibrium solvents. Such an approach is also called a state-specific approach. It is implemented in the software package Gaussian09 and is a method for determining the emission energy and reorganization energy of the solvent.

Under the two-state approximation, the electronic coupling is determined using the generalized Mulliken-Hush (GMH) approach (Cui and Xue, 2022; Zheng et al., 2005):
(7)
V
if
'
=
Δ E
μ
if
(
μ i
-
μ f
)
2
+ 4
μ
if
2
Here,
μ
if
is the transition dipole moment between the excited and ground states;
μ i
is the dipole moment of excited state and
μ f
is the dipole moment of ground state.

In addition to the nonradiative decay rate, we also calculate the radiative decay rate, which together with the nonradiative decay rate (
k r
) defines the quantum yield (QY) of GQDs according to the following equation (Cui and Xue, 2022):
(8)
k r
=
n
e
2
Δ E
f
osc
2 π ε
m e
ℏ
2
c
3
(9)
QY =
k r
k r
+
k
nr
where e is elementary charge; n is refractive index;
f
osc
is the oscillator strength obtained with time-dependent DFT (TDDFT) calculations; ε is dielectric constant of solvent;
m e
is the rest mass of electron; c is light speed.

The B3LYP functional was used to optimize the initial structures of the doped and undoped GQDs. 6-31g* basis set was applied to C, H and O atoms of GQDs. Such a combination well reproduces the photophysical properties of the conjugated molecular system and agrees with experimental results (Lu et al., 2021; Lu et al., 2021). Based on Eqs. (3)-(5), frequency calculations were performed to obtain the intramolecular reorganization energy, and the effective Huang-Rhys factor and vibrational frequency. The TDDFT calculations were performed to calculate excitation energies which were subsequently Gaussian broadening with a linewidth of 0.08 eV to obtain the absorption spectra.

The natural transition orbitals (NTOs) were obtained by a unitary transformation of the canonical molecular orbitals, and plotted using Chemcraft software (Zhurko and Zhurko, 2009). All calculations were performed in dichloromethane using the conductor-like polarizable continuum model (CPCM) (Barone and Cossi, 1998).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Electronic structure and optical properties of P-functionalized GQDs
ce:section.ce:para: Experimental studies show that four types of P-functional groups mainly passivate the edges of GQDs, namely phosphine oxide (OPX3), phosphinic acid (PO2H), phosphate (PO4H2), and phosphonic acid (PO3H2) (Li et al., 2017; Hasegawa et al., 2015; Xia et al., 2017) (Fig. 1). These P-functional groups form the sp3 bonds at the periphery of the GQDs and disrupt the integrity of the sp2 network in the conjugated π-system. They also contain a different number of PO double bonds, which extends the range of the π-electron system in GQDs. Compared with the four P-functional groups, PO2H has the strongest influence on the sp2 bond environment of GQDs because the phosphorus is a double-site dopant by forming two sp3 bonds with the neighboring carbon atoms of P-GQDs. In contrast, the phosphorus atom in the other P-functional groups is a single-site dopant with a single sp3 bond, which has relatively little influence on the sp2 bond environment of the GQDs. The strong influence of PO2H on the sp2 network in GQDs leads to deformation of GQD structures at high passivation densities (Fig. 2 (l)-(o)). In contrast, GQDs functionalized with OPX3, PO4H2, and PO3H2 groups retain their planar geometries, which are independent of structural symmetries and edge coverages. This is very similar to nitrated GQDs (Sheely et al., 2021), where the addition of NO2 groups deforms the structure of GQDs at high passivation densities. However, in the case of NO2, the strong electron attraction causes electron localization leading to deformation of the planar structure of the GQDs. In contrast, the PO2H group distorts the planar structure of GQDs by mixing sp2 and sp3 bonding environments. Both functional groups cause electron localization by breaking the integrity of the conjugated π-system in GQDs.

In experimental systems, the exact locations and passivation density of functional groups are uncertain. Therefore, the first goal of this research is to determine the location of the functional group GQDs. There are two non-equivalent positions on the GQD edges, referred to as A1 and A2 (Fig. 1 (g)). Our calculations show that the A1 position is thermodynamically more favorable than the A2 position for all P-functionalized GQDs (supplemental Table S1). Therefore, we choose the A1 position at each edge of the GQD as the functionalization site for all molecular systems.

The average C-C bond lengths at the edges decrease with the increase in the number of PO2H (supplemental Figure S1), whereas they are insignificantly affected by the passivation of OPX3, PO3H2, and PO4H2 groups. This is due to the mixing of the sp3 bonding environment of the P-functional groups with the sp2 bonding environment at the edges of the GQDs. Since the sp3 bonding environment in PO2H-functionalized GQDs has a greater influence on the sp2 bonding environment than in the other three molecular systems, this leads to a stronger reduction of the CC bond lengths at the edges. OPX3 is linked to the GQD edge via a P atom with a PC bond length (d(P-C)) of 1.88 Å. In the case of PO3H2 and PO2H, d(P-C) is 1.82 Å and 1.77 Å, respectively. PO4H2 is connected to the GQD edge via an oxygen atom with an OC bond length of 1.40 Å, and the average PO bond length of the other three PO bonds is 1.66 Å, which agrees well with a previous report (López-Urías et al., 2021). The PO bond length in these functional groups is 1.50 Å, similar to that in phosphorus groups (Feng et al., 2019).

Edge coverage affects not only the physical structure of PO2H-functionalized GQDs but also their electronic structures. The bandgaps are significantly reduced in PO2H-functionalized GQDs (Fig. 3 ) compared to the undoped GQD ((supplemental Figure S2)), while they are less reduced in the other three P-functionalized GQDs. In the first scenario, the mixing of the sp2 and sp3 bonding environments stabilizes the LUMO orbital and destabilizes the HOMO orbital, causing a localization of the electron density in the PO2H-functionalized GQDs and thus reducing the bandgap (Sheely et al., 2021). In the second scenario, the PO double bond extends the π-electron domain of the GQDs, shifts the energy levels of the HOMO and LUMO orbitals downward, and breaks their degeneracies (Li et al., 2015). The larger energy shift of the LUMO orbital than that of the HOMO orbital leads to a decrease in the bandgap. In addition, the LUMO levels of functionalized GQDs vary between −2.5 to −2.2 eV, which are higher than the CBM of TiO2 (−4.0 eV), ensuring a proper thermodynamic condition for electron injection from the GQD to the TiO2. With the exception of the PO2H-functionalized GQDs, the HOMO levels of the functionalized GQDs are lower than the potential of the redox couple (−4.8 eV), which ensures fast regeneration of the ground state of the GQDs.

Moreover, the disruption of the integrity of the conjugated system leads to the localization of sp2 electrons in the P-GQDs. When the carbon skeleton of the GQDs is passivated with one PO2H group or two PO2H groups at symmetric sites, this effect has little impact on the regularity of the electron distribution in the carbon skeleton (supplemental Figure S2 (f) and (g)). However, when the PO2H groups are arranged asymmetrically (supplemental Figure S2 (h) and (j)), the frontier molecular orbitals (FMOs) are localized near the GQD edge. This is because the asymmetric passivation disturbs the chemical symmetry of the GQDs and causes local dipoles at their edges, leading to localization of electron density and non-uniform electron distribution. Moreover, an increase in the passivation density of the PO2H group increases the localization of electron density in the FMOs due to the greater influence of the sp3 bonding environment on the conjugated π-system (supplemental Figure S2 (i)).

Note that the decrease in structural symmetry of the GQDs decreases their dipole moments when they are bound with two OPX3 groups, whereas it increases the dipole moment of the GQDs when they are passivated with four OPX3 groups (supplemental Figure S2 (b)-(e)). In the case of PO3H2-functionalized GQDs, the decrease in the structural symmetry of the GQDs decreases the dipole moments, regardless of whether the passivation density is higher or lower. However, the dipole moments of OPH2-functionalized GQDs show lower sensitivity to structural symmetry at high passivation density (supplemental Figure S2 (i) and (j)). Moreover, the dipole moments of PO4H2-functionalized GQDs show lower sensitivity to both structural symmetry and passivation density. The discrepancy in the dipole moments of P-GQDs with respect to their structural symmetries and edge coverages is caused by the permanent dipoles formed at the GQD edges. The carbon atom has a higher electronegativity than the hydrogen atom; therefore, permanent dipoles form at the edges of GQDs. The passivation of P-functional groups at the GQD edges disturbs the electron density distribution at the GQD edges, resulting in a variation of their dipole moments. This variation depends on the species, bond positions, and passivation densities of the P functionalities. This is very similar to GQDs functionalized with O functional groups with different electron-withdrawing abilities (Sheely et al., 2021). The O functional group, which has a strong electronegativity, leads to a redistribution of the electron density in the carbon skeleton, which can be manipulated by different passivation densities and bond positions.

Compared with the pristine GQD (supplemental Figure S3), P-functionalization causes a red shift of the main absorption band in the absorption spectra (Fig. 4 ). In particular, the PO2H functionalization causes a larger red shift than the other P-functionalized systems, which is consistent with the previously determined electronic structures. The main absorption band in pristine GQD is doubly degenerate (Li et al., 2015). The P-functionalization breaks the degenerate bright states in the pristine GQD, leading to the formation of low-energy bright states. This is similar to N-doped GQDs (Niu et al., 2016) and P,N-codoped GQDs (Feng et al., 2021), where the different N- and P-doping configurations break the degeneracy of the main absorption peaks, leading to the formation of low-energy peaks. The number and intensity of these low-energy peaks depend on the species, symmetry, and passivation density of the P functional groups bound to the GQDs. Moreover, more low-energy states are split off from the main absorption band in PO2H-functionalized GQDs than in the other P-GQDs, which is due to the greater influence of the sp3 bonding environment on the delocalized sp2 electrons. Furthermore, P-functionalization effectively eliminates the low-lying dark states, which become bright states. The intensity of these bright states depends on the structural symmetry and edge coverage of the GQDs.

It is worth mentioning that the enlargement in the sizes of the GQDs decreases the quantum confinement and increases the delocalization of the sp2 electrons. As a result, the bandgap narrows, leading to a red shift in the absorption spectra. The bandgaps and optical properties of GQDs correlate closely with the edge states of zigzag-edged GQDs (Sk et al., 2014). Consequently, the edge coverage has a greater influence on the localized states generated by P-functionalization as well as on the optoelectronic properties of GQDs than the GQD size.

The NTOs of the lowest excited states in five molecular systems were plotted to study the distribution of electrons and holes in pristine and P-GQDs (Fig. 5 ). The NTOs of the GQDs functionalized with OPX3, PO3H2, and PO4H2 groups (Fig. 5 (a)-(e), (k)-(t)) resemble the NTOs of the pristine GQDs (Fig. 5 (u)), while the NTOs of the PO2H-functionalized GQDs show considerable disorder of the conjugated π-system due to the strong mixing of sp3- with sp2-bonding environments. The distribution of electrons and holes is increasingly localized at the GQD edges when the passivation density of the PO2H group is increased (Fig. 5 (f)-(j)), which is consistent with the previous report (Feng et al., 2019), while in other P-functionalized GQDs the electrons and holes are distributed over the carbon skeleton.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Energy conversion performance of P-functionalized GQDs
ce:section.ce:para: In experiments, passivation of the ligand leads to strong dipole moments in the GQDs. Therefore, we investigate the energy conversion performance in the asymmetric GQDs structures bound by different P functional groups. The optical gap of the GQDs is increased by increasing the structural symmetry, which improves the uniformity of electron dispersion in the GQDs and increases the energy barrier for the nonradiative transition. As a result of the increased energy barrier, the nonradiative decay is reduced.

First, the nonradiative decay rate of the pristine GQDs was calculated (supplemental Table S2) and compared with experimental value (Mueller et al., 2011) to see if Eq. (1) was appropriate for the simulated system. We compare the optical gap and nonradiative decay rate of the
S 3
state with the experimental data because the first two optically dark states in pristine GQDs cannot be detected in the experiment. The estimated optical gap correlates well with the experimentally determined optical gap of first optically active state. Moreover, the estimated nonradiative decay rate of the
S 3
state is in good agreement with the experimental value of the first absorption peak, indicating that the rate Eq. (1) is suitable for calculating the nonradiative decay rate in GQD materials.

The photoexcited states are localized and fluctuate in energy due to the vibrational motions of the atoms. This causes the system to transition from the upper diabatic potential surface to the lower diabatic potential surface, releasing electronic energy and converting it to phonons. Typically, certain nuclear vibrational modes have a greater influence on such an electronic transition than the other vibrational modes. Therefore, the active nuclear modes of GQDs have different coupling strengths with nonradiative electronic transition at different vibrational frequencies.

The solvent reorganization energy of GQD-4PO2H′ is significantly larger than the other three molecular systems, suggesting that the mixing of sp2 and sp3 bonding environments causes a significant change of excited-state surface potential. This increases the difference in dipole moments between the excited state and the ground state, resulting in a larger reorganization energy of the solvent (Improta et al., 2006). The mixing of sp2 and sp3 bonding environments increases not only the dipole difference but also the structural flexibility of the GQDs, leading to the largest intramolecular reorganization energy in GQD-4PO2H′ among all GQDs. The GQD-4PO3H2′ has the second largest solvent reorganization energy, followed by the GQD-4PO4H2′, indicating that the PC bond induces more changes in dipole moments than the PO bond. On the other hand, the intramolecular reorganization energy of GQD-4PO4H2′ is larger than that of GQD-4PO3H2′. This is because the phosphonic acid is more stable than the phosphonate (Sevrain et al., 2017) and therefore reduces the structural flexibility of GQDs. The electronic coupling of GQD-4PO3H2′ is about ten times greater than the other molecular systems, while it is the smallest in the case of GQD-4PO4H2′. This indicates that the PC bond at the periphery of GQDs decreases the distance between electrons and holes, dramatically increasing their interaction in the conjugated π-system of GQDs.

Several factors influence the values of nonradiative decay rates, such as the Huang-Rhys factor, the emission energy, the electronic coupling and the reorganization energy according to equations (1) and (2). The electronic coupling is proportional to the nonradiative decay rate according to the rate equation (1). Increasing the reorganization energy while decreasing the emission energy increases the last exponential term in equation (2), which accordingly reduces the potential energy barrier to the nonradiative decay (Cui and Xue, 2021). A larger effective Huang-Rhys factor accelerates the nonradiative transition between excited and ground states because the larger effective vibrational frequency corresponds to a larger nonadiabatic coupling vector. The larger the nonadiabatic coupling vector, the faster the nonradiative relaxation proceeds (Cui and Xue, 2022).

The calculated nonradiative decay rates are GQD-4PO3H2′ > GQD-4PO2H′ > GQD-4OPX3′ > GQD-4PO4H2′. Although the phosphonic acid is structurally similar to the phosphonate group, the nonradiative decay rates of the two P-GQDs differ dramatically. This is because the PC bond at the periphery of GQD-4PO3H2′ drastically enhances electron-hole interaction while the PO bond capped at the edge of the GQDs greatly reduces the electron-hole interaction, which accordingly leads to the largest electronic coupling in GQD-4PO3H2′ and the smallest electronic coupling in GQD-4PO4H2′. Moreover, the PC bond at the periphery of GQD-4PO3H2′ increases the solvent and intramolecular reorganization energies compared to the PO bond at the periphery of GQD-4PO4H2′, which also contribute to faster nonradiative decay. Although GQD-4PO2H′ has the largest solvent and intramolecular reorganization energies, its electronic coupling is much smaller than that of GQD-4PO3H2′; therefore, its nonradiative decay rate is smaller than that of GQD-4PO3H2′. However, the non-radiative decay rate of GQD-4PO2H′ is larger than that of GQD-4OPX3′ due to its larger solvent and intramolecular reorganization energies. On the other hand, the calculated radiative rates are GQD-4PO3H2′ > GQD-4OPX3′ > GQD-4PO2H′ > GQD-4PO4H2′. The GQD-4PO3H2′ has the largest radiative decay rate due to the larger emission energy and oscillator strength than other systems. The GQD-4PO4H2′ has the smallest radiative decay rate owning to its much smaller oscillator strength.

In terms of QYs, the lowest energetic state is typically the dark state in pristine GQDs with vanishingly small QYs (Niu et al., 2016). P-doping leads to a brightening of the dark state in the GQDs and increases the QYs. The calculated QYs vary between 0.0001 ∼ 3 % depending on the specific doping configuration of the P functionality. The GQD-4OPX3′ and GQD-4PO4H2′ have relatively larger QYs than the others. GQD-4OPX3′ has the second largest radiative rate and the second smallest nonradiative rate, resulting in the largest QY. For GQD-4PO4H2′, both the radiative rate and the nonradiative rate are the smallest among all P-functionalized GQDs. However, the nonradiative rate is two orders of magnitude smaller than the radiative rate, resulting in the second largest QY among all P-functionalized GQDs. GQD-4PO3H2′ has the smallest QY of nearly zero due to the largest nonradiative decay rate.

Fast nonradiative recombination is detrimental to the energy conversion performance of a light sensitizer because it reduces the short-circuit current density (Zhang et al., 2012). Moreover, the open-circuit voltage, fill factor, light harvesting efficiency and charge injection from the excited states of GQDs into TiO2 play a crucial role in regulating the energy conversion of GQDs. Therefore, we further calculate Voc, FF, ΔGinject and LHE to obtain a comprehensive evaluation of the energy conversion performance of P-functionalized GQDs (Table 2 ). The results show: the Voc, FF and ΔGinject are GQD-4PO4H2′ > GQD-4OPX3′ > GQD-4PO3H2′ > GQD-4PO2H′, and the LHE are GQD-4OPX3′ > GQD-4PO4H2′ ≈ GQD-4PO3H2′ > GQD-4PO2H′. Therefore, the GQDs functionalized with phosphate and phosphine oxide have better energy conversion performance than the other GQDs because they have higher open-circuit voltage, larger fill factor, larger driving force for charge injection, slower nonradiative recombination, and higher light collection efficiency. The phosphinic acid- and phosphonic acid-functionalized GQDs have relatively lower energy conversion performance than the other two GQDs. In the case of phosphinic acid-functionalized GQD, the lowest Voc, FF, ΔGinject and LHE, and the higher nonradiative recombination rate than the phosphate- and phosphine oxide-functionalized GQDs lead to lower energy conversion, while in the case of phosphonic acid-functionalized GQD, the fastest nonradiative recombination rate contributes to its lower energy conversion. Overall, the phosphate- and phosphine oxide-functionalized GQDs show better energy conversion efficiency and QYs than the other P-functionalized systems and are more suitable for photovoltaic devices.

In the present work, we investigate the effects of four different P functional groups (i.e., OPX3, PO2H, PO3H2, and PO4H2) on the optoelectronic properties of GQDs with different structural symmetries and edge coverages by performing DFT and TDDFT calculations. The phosphorus atom in the PO2H group is a double-site dopant that forms sp3 bonds with two adjacent carbon atoms at the periphery of the P-GQDs. This has a much stronger influence on the sp2 bond environment of the carbon skeleton of the P-GQDs, leading to a much stronger reduction of the average C-C bond lengths at the GQD edges and deformation of the planar structures of the GQDs at the high passivation densities of the PO2H groups. Moreover, the bandgaps of the PO2H-functionalized GQDs were more reduced than those of the other P-GQDs due to the mixing of the sp2- with the sp3-bond environments. The mixing of the sp3-bond environment with the sp2-bond environment also leads to a redistribution of the electron density in the carbon skeleton of the P-GQDs, which affects their dipole moments which are sensitive to the structural symmetry and edge coverage of the GQDs.

The absorption spectra of the P-GQDs are red-shifted compared to the pristine GQD due to electron localization, which reduces their bandgaps. The P-functionalization also leads to the appearance of low absorption peaks in P-GQDs because the degeneracy of the main absorption peak is broken. In particular, the PO2H-functionalized GQDs have more low-energy peaks split off from the main absorption peaks, which is due to the stronger influence of the sp3 bonding environment on the sp2 bonding environment in the carbon skeleton. Moreover, the electron and hole densities are increasingly localized at the GQD edge as the passivation density of the PO2H group is increased.

The nonradiative decay rate of PO3H2-functionalized GQD is much larger than that of PO4H2-functionalized GQD because the PC bond increases the electronic coupling and reorganization energies of P-GQDs. Moreover, the larger solvent and intramolecular reorganization energies leads to faster nonradiative decay in PO2H-functionalized GQD than in OPX3-functionalized GQD. The overall photovoltaic performance of PO4H2- and OPX3-functionalized GQDs are better than the other P-functionalized GQDs in terms of energy conversion and QYs.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.solener.2022.07.028.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1
